notes.anchor: beyond whatever sum the law allows to be laid out on a monument, an equal sum must be given to the people
notes.note: Roman sumptuary law capped what could be spent on a funerary monument; Cicero has just learned that exceeding the cap obliged the builder to pay a matching sum into the public purse. The cost is what now drives him to consider changing the site.

notes.anchor: I would not have it called by any name except a shrine
notes.note: Cicero insists the structure for Tullia be a fanum (shrine), implying her consecration and quasi-deification, not a mere tomb (sepulcrum) subject to the monument law. He concedes the wish is alogōs, irrational, but it governs the whole search for a site.
